title: Ongoing Compliance and Your Action Plan
learningObjectives: Create a personal 30-Day Action Plan to apply what you've learned
Identify institutional resources for ongoing accessibility support
Understand how accessibility standards evolve and how to stay current

keyTerms.definition: A structured, four-week plan starting immediately after completing this course. Each week focuses on a specific accessibility priority (syllabus, quizzes, video, and sharing with a colleague).
A concrete, time-bounded list of specific changes a faculty member commits to making to their course materials, with deadlines and accountability.

The campus office responsible for compliance with accessibility laws (often called Accessibility Services, Disability Services, or the Office for Students with Disabilities). A resource for ongoing training, tool access, and escalation.
A group of faculty and staff who meet regularly to discuss accessibility, share solutions, and learn from each other. Some institutions have formal accessibility communities; others are informal.
The next major version of the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, currently in development by the W3C. Scheduled for completion in 2025-2026. Will likely introduce new concepts and criteria.
Recognition from your institution (sometimes as a certificate, sometimes counting toward tenure or promotion requirements) for completing formal accessibility training like this course.

contentBlocks.title: Your 30-Day Action Plan
contentBlocks.body:
You've learned a lot in this course. The question now is: what are you going to *do* with it?

Research on behavior change shows that intention alone doesn't stick. You need a plan with specific, time-bounded actions. That's why this section asks you to create a **30-Day Action Plan** — a concrete commitment to make one meaningful accessibility change per week for the next month.

Here's the framework:

**Week 1: Fix Your Syllabus and One High-Use Document**
- Select your syllabus (or course handbook if that's more relevant).
- Run the Microsoft Accessibility Checker (or use your institution's tool).
- Fix at least the top three issues: heading structure, any missing alt text, link text clarity.
- Also pick one other document you use in every class (a handout, a lab template, a reading guide). Apply the 60-Second Scan. Fix at least one issue.
- Time estimate: 45-60 minutes.
- **Why this week first?** The syllabus is the most-read document in your course. If it's accessible, you've made an immediate impact.

**Week 2: Audit Your Quizzes with Keyboard Testing**
- Pick 2-3 quizzes you use regularly.
- For each one, navigate it using keyboard only (Tab, Enter, Space — no mouse).
- Note any questions that fail the keyboard test (e.g., drag-and-drop, image hotspots).
- Rebuild at least one inaccessible question type as an accessible alternative (drag-and-drop → dropdown; hotspot → multiple choice with descriptions).
- Time estimate: 30-45 minutes.
- **Why this week?** Quizzes are high-stakes assessments. If a student can't navigate your quiz, it's both an accessibility barrier and a validity problem.

**Week 3: Check All Videos for Captions**
- Make a list of every video you show or require students to watch: your lectures, YouTube videos, publisher videos, documentaries.
- Spot-check 5-10 of them for captions. Note which ones don't have captions.
- For your own recorded lectures, commit to a captioning method going forward (YouTube auto-captions, Otter.ai, Microsoft Stream auto-captions, or manual captions). Set a reminder to caption the next video you record.
- Time estimate: 20-30 minutes for the audit; then ongoing 5-10 minutes per new video.
- **Why this week?** Video is high-engagement content. A student who is deaf or hard-of-hearing cannot access uncaptioned video. This is high-impact and legally required under Title II.

**Week 4: Share What You've Learned**
- Schedule a 15-20 minute conversation with a colleague — another faculty member in your department, a friend from your discipline, or your department chair.
- Share one thing you learned in this course that surprised you.
- Show them one accessibility fix you made (fixed heading structure, alt text example, caption check).
- Ask: "What's the biggest accessibility barrier you see in your own courses?" Listen to their answer.
- **Why this week?** Accessibility becomes normal when it's part of professional conversation. You're modeling it and lowering barriers for someone else to learn.

**After 30 days,** you'll have made four concrete improvements. Equally important: you'll have built four new habits that are easier to repeat next semester. Accessibility won't feel like an extra thing you have to do — it'll be part of how you teach.

contentBlocks.title: Staying Current Without Drowning
contentBlocks.body:
Accessibility standards evolve. WCAG 2.2 just became the standard in October 2023. WCAG 3.0 is [in development](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/) and will likely be released in 2025 or 2026. As a faculty member, you don't need to become an expert in every new standard. But you do need a way to stay informed without making accessibility a second job.

Here's a realistic approach to staying current:

**Follow 2-3 accessibility blogs or sources.** You don't need more than this. Pick one or two and subscribe to their newsletter or RSS feed. You'll get 2-3 emails per month about accessibility news, new tools, or guideline changes. It takes 5 minutes to skim.

Good options:
- [WebAIM](https://webaim.org/) — straightforward, practical, faculty-friendly
- [The A11y Project](https://www.a11yproject.com/) — broader accessibility community news
- [AHEAD (Association on Higher Education and Disability)](https://www.ahead.org/) — specifically for higher-ed accessibility professionals

Your institution might also have an accessibility office that sends monthly tips or hosts brown-bag sessions. Sign up for those.

**Attend your institution's accessibility training** — if offered. Most institutions offer a one-hour orientation or workshop on campus resources. One hour per year keeps you connected and reminds you where to go for help.

**Join a community of practice if one exists.** Some campuses have faculty accessibility circles where 8-10 faculty meet monthly to share struggles and solutions. If your institution doesn't have one, consider starting one — literally just a monthly coffee chat, or a Slack channel. The peer learning is invaluable.

**Be clear about what you don't need to know.** You don't need to memorize all 86 WCAG success criteria. You don't need to become a web developer or learn HTML. You don't need to attend every webinar or read every accessibility paper. You need to know: (1) the basics you've learned in this course, (2) where your institutional resources are, and (3) who to ask when you're confused. That's it.

**Expect standards to change.** When WCAG 3.0 arrives, it might introduce new concepts. Your institution will likely offer training on how it affects higher ed. There might be a transition period. Don't panic. Your accessibility skills will still apply — you'll just have new tools or guidelines to learn. Accessibility is a discipline that evolves. That's normal and okay.

contentBlocks.title: From This Course to Your Institution
contentBlocks.body:
Here are concrete ways to leverage this course in your institutional context:

**Use your completion certificate.** When you finish this course, you'll have a certificate showing you've completed a formal accessibility training. Save it. If your institution has a requirement for professional development hours, this might count. If you're working toward tenure or promotion, having documented accessibility training strengthens your teaching portfolio. If your department is implementing accessibility initiatives, bring the certificate to relevant meetings — it shows you're trained and ready to help.

**Advocate for department-wide training.** You've completed this course. Your colleagues haven't. If you work in a department where accessibility hasn't been a priority, consider asking your department chair: "Could we require all faculty to complete an accessibility orientation?" or "Could we host a brown-bag session where I share what I learned?" One trained faculty member can be the catalyst for broader change.

**Work with your Center for Teaching and Learning (CTL).** Your CTL likely runs faculty workshops. They care about teaching effectiveness. Accessibility *is* teaching effectiveness — it means all students can access your materials. Reach out to your CTL director and say: "I just completed an accessibility course. Would CTL be interested in including accessibility in our fall faculty workshop?" or "Could I offer a one-hour accessibility session for your teaching certificate program?" CTLs are usually excited to expand their offerings, and you now have the knowledge to contribute.

**Connect with Disability Services.** If you haven't already, introduce yourself to your campus Disability Services office. Tell them you completed this accessibility course and you're committed to building accessible materials. Ask if they have any requests ("Are there document types we see repeatedly that are inaccessible?") or resources they recommend (tool access, templates, guidelines). This relationship will pay off — they become your go-to resource when you have questions, and you become someone they can point faculty to when other courses have accessibility issues.

**Lead by example.** The most powerful advocacy for accessibility is doing it. Build accessible courses. When colleagues ask how you're managing it, tell them. When a student thanks you for captions or accessible documents, let others know it was a priority. When your institution celebrates you for accessibility leadership (and they will, if you're visible), accept the recognition and talk about how the training made it possible.

**The business case for your institution.** If you ever make a case to your administration for more accessibility resources or training, here's the argument: faculty accessibility training reduces complaints to Disability Services, reduces OCR violations, and improves outcomes for students with disabilities. It also helps all students (captions help non-native English speakers, large text helps aging faculty, clear writing helps everyone). It's good for compliance *and* good for students.

Your 30-Day Action Plan structures the next month: Week 1 fix your syllabus and a key document, Week 2 test quizzes, Week 3 check videos, Week 4 share what you've learned. Stay current by following 2-3 accessibility sources and attending your institution's trainings. Don't try to memorize all standards — know where to ask for help. Use your completion certificate for professional development. Connect with Disability Services and your CTL. Lead by example.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Write your personal 30-Day Action Plan. Be specific: which documents will you fix in Week 1? Which quizzes will you test in Week 2? Which videos will you check in Week 3? Who will you share this with in Week 4? Make it concrete and realistic for your actual teaching load. Also describe one longer-term accessibility goal you have for your courses beyond the 30 days.
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response includes concrete, course-specific details for each week — actual document names, quiz titles, or video types rather than generic placeholders. It shows realistic understanding of the faculty member's workload and constraints. The Week 4 colleague might be a specific person or role they've identified. The response includes at least one longer-term goal that connects back to learning from the course (e.g., "I want to build an accessible template library," "I want to make sure all my assessments are keyboard-navigable," "I want to work with my department on adoption of an accessible syllabus template").
